data.title: Novo Nordisk: Is The GLP‑1 Golden Age Already Behind It?
data.description:
Elevator pitch

I see a lot of challenges with Novo Nordisk (NVO) (NOVO:CA) (NONOF):

  1. Novo Nordisk's share in the obesity and diabetes market is eroding
  2. Pricing headwinds are leading to margin deterioration
  3. Patent expirations are another headwind to sales and profits
  4. Undervaluation of NVO stock is an upside risk
  5. I expect the downtrend to continue

Novo Nordisk's share in the obesity and diabetes market is eroding in most markets

Novo Nordisk has steadily been losing market share in its key markets in the broader diabetes medication market and GLP-1 drugs:

You may note in the above charts that China is an exception for the company, as it is gaining share here. However, this is a minuscule portion of overall revenues:

A key reason for this share loss is that Eli Lilly (LLY) has been a formidable contender with its Zepbound and Mounjaro GLP-1 drugs:

The company has ceded U.S. market leadership to Eli Lilly (LLY), whose triple-digit growth in Zepbound and Mounjaro enabled it to capture roughly 57% of U.S. GLP-1 prescriptions by late 2025.

- Seeking Alpha news

Novo Nordisk has also made some unforced errors, as its latest weight-loss drug, CagriSema, underperformed Eli Lilly's Zepbound (which uses the tirzepatide active ingredient) in clinical trials:

...people treated with CagriSema 2.4 mg/2.4 mg achieved a weight loss of 20.2% compared to 23.6% with tirzepatide at 84 weeks. The trial did not achieve its primary endpoint of demonstrating non-inferiority on weight loss for CagriSema compared to tirzepatide after 84 weeks.

- Novo Nordisk

However, there is a silver lining because a study published last week shows Novo Nordisk's GLP-1 active ingredient of semaglutide to be better at preserving patients' lean muscle mass compared to Eli Lilly's drugs:

peer review, were based on 1,800 patients on tirzepatide and 6,200 on semaglutide whose lean body mass [LBM] measurements were taken before and after they initiated the respective GLP-1 drugs.

According to the study, patients on tirzepatide indicated 1.1%, 1.5%, 1.3%, and 2.0% greater LBM loss at 3, 6, 9, and 12 months, respectively, compared to those on semaglutide...

- Analytics firm nference, Seeking Alpha

So as the market matures, I expect doctors and patients to opt for the drug that preserves muscle better, as that is linked to better overall health and longevity outcomes.

Pricing headwinds are leading to margin deterioration

Novo Nordisk has been posting larger misses in EBITDA margins vs. consensus estimates recently:

This is due to more intense competition and the US government's pricing interventions via the Most Favored Nations (MFN) pricing policy, which matches US drug prices to that of the lowest prices in peer nations.

I expect these headwinds to continue along with the additional headwind of patent expirations (discussed in the following section) to put further pressure on gross margins:

Patent expirations are another headwind to sales and profits

The key active ingredient, semaglutide, in Novo Nordisk's GLP-1 drugs Ozempic and Wegovy has lost patent expirations in some key markets such as Canada, Brazil, China, and India. This has opened the floodgates to low-cost generic alternatives. For example, in India, many Indian pharmaceutical companies are launching GLP-1 generics at $14 a month. For context, in the US, the cost is $199 to $349 a month.

Now, India makes up a small portion of NVO's overall sales currently. But the risk here is that patent expirations can cannibalize sales and profits in other large markets such as China (~5-6% of overall sales).

Note that both Ozempic and Wegovy have already seen a steady deceleration or even declining sales:

I expect to see further sales stagnancy and decline for these drugs over the medium term.

Undervaluation of NVO stock is an upside risk

NVO is trading at a 1-yr PE of 12.4x, which is 18% below the sectoral comps' median multiple of 15.2x:

This discount valuation for NVO vs. its peers has not been normal, as the stock has typically traded at a relative 5.6% premium over the last year:

Relative to its longer-term 1-year forward PEs as well, NVO's multiple now is at a 42% discount to the longer-term median of 21.4x.

Hence, I perceive some upside valuation risk to the stock.

I expect the downtrend to continue

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using technical analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

NVO Technical Analysis

The monthly chart of NVO stock shows an overall downtrend and a recent strong bearish engulfing candle. Due to this seller momentum, I expect more downside after a momentary pullback up.

Takeaway and Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bearish due to declining market shares along with broad-based sales and margin headwinds.
  • Valuation: Bullish, as NVO stock seems undervalued from multiple perspectives.
  • Technical Analysis: Bearish due to strong selling pressure.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bearish, Sell

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DXP Enterprises: Organic Growth Outpaces M&A, Margins Improve (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In February, after a weak Q3 report, I recommended tactically holding stocks and waiting for the release of the Q4 report, as there were concerns that the report might be weaker than expected. However, DXP Enterprises, Inc. (DXPE) was pleased with the advance of the consensus forecast for both revenue and EPS. Against the background of a good report, the stock has grown even more, and since my Buy recommendation in October last year, the total growth has been about 27%. The strategy chosen by the management to diversify the business and reduce dependence on oil and gas makes revenue more stable and more predictable. At the same time, due to the growth of financial indicators, the multiples have decreased slightly, and for many of them, the company's shares are now trading at a discount to the sectoral median values. Considering the financial situation and the current multiplier rating, I am upgrading the rating from Hold to Buy. According to my estimate, by building a DCF model, the fair value of the shares is $185 in the baseline scenario with an upside of 14.5% to the current price ($161.7 at the time of writing).

Strong Quarter Driven by Organic Growth and IPS Expansion

The latest key event in the company's business is related to the release of the Q4 financial statements and the market's reaction to the demonstrated results. I liked the results of the published report. The company managed to beat market expectations in terms of revenue and EPS. I would like to analyze key aspects of the reporting so that further forecasts for Q1 from the next chapter (the report is due out in May) are more understandable to the reader. But before that, I recommend that you read my first two articles about the company, where I have analyzed in detail all the company's activities, its business model, and its development strategy.

Revenue for the quarter increased by 12% yoy to $527.4 million. For comparison, I predicted about $500 million due to fewer working days and a seasonal slowdown in business by the end of the year, and the consensus forecast was $499 million, which is almost 6% lower than the actual result. Compared to Q3, revenue increased by 2.7%. An important caveat is that revenue from the core business grew strongly this quarter, and only part of the revenue was added by the absorbed companies. Organic revenue increased by as much as 16% to $505.5 million, and additional revenue from new assets even decreased by $13 million. This indicates a high demand for the company's solutions, as evidenced by the growth of sales per business day increased by 12% yoy to $8.5 million.

The situation has not changed by segment for several consecutive quarters—two key segments are growing at double-digit rates, while the third continues to stagnate. Revenue at Service Centers increased by 14.6% yoy to $356.2 million. In the 10-K, management points to high demand and the growth of large orders for adhesives in some regions, as well as growing interest in metalworking and air compressors. IPS revenue grew even faster, by 12.7%, to $110.0 million. IPS is more marginal, and high revenue growth rates help to increase the overall marginality of the business. According to management, the segment's revenue increased mainly due to new large client projects, as well as continued diversification into water and wastewater. But SCS sales have not grown, which has been attributed to lower activity from oil and gas customers for several quarters in a row. For investors, it is more important that the first two segments grow, and if IPS is growing faster than Service Centers, then this is doubly good, since the operating margin is growing along with it.

That's what happened in the end—operating profit increased by 18.8% yoy to $46.7 million. Profit growth faster than revenue resulted in an improvement in the operating margin from 8.3% to 8.9%. The adjusted EBITDA margin also increased to 11.2% from 10.7%. The IPS segment became the main engine of growth, where the operating margin increased from 15.6% to 18.0%. Operating expenses grew in Q4, but higher sales growth at Service Centers and IPS helped boost overall margins. The situation turned out to be slightly lower at the net profit level, but due to a one-time strong increase in the effective tax rate from 6% to 26%. As a result, the net margin decreased to 4.3% from 4.5%. Adjusted EPS rose from $1.29 to $1.39, with a consensus forecast of $1.30. I was close in my forecast, expecting $1.40 EPS. EPS growth was marginally impacted by share buybacks, but otherwise all the growth was due to last year's weak results and strong operating results in Q4’2026.

Overall, the company's report turned out to be strong, except for a nuance related to a strong increase in tax expenses. But even with this in mind, all indicators have grown at a faster pace, primarily due to organic business growth, not acquisitions. If DXP continues at the same pace, the stock will inevitably rise by another 10-20%.

Consensus and Management Aligned on Growth Outlook

Management does not make forecasts, either quarterly or yearly. From the management's comments, it can be concluded that they expect both organic growth in both business segments and acquisition-driven growth to continue. The situation with oil and gas is also expected to improve in the second half of the year. In the 10-K, the company writes that it expects growth rates similar to those in 2025, but subject to maintaining a favorable macroeconomic environment. Interest expenses are also expected to rise, as the company needs financing to maintain its growth rate. This will put pressure on net income, but it will reduce tax expenses.

The market consensus for the full year 2026 assumes revenue growth of 10.20% yoy to $2.22 billion and EPS of 12.92% yoy to $6.12. From the first quarter, the market expects revenue growth of 11.53% yoy to $531.5 million and EPS growth of 2.38% yoy to $1.29. Market expectations generally agree with management's comments. According to my expectations, the company will deliver a strong first quarter due to high demand in the two largest segments. I expect the following results for Q1: revenue of $532-535 million and EPS of $1.28-1.30.

Conservative DCF Still Shows Upside

Let's move on to the main thing, namely the assessment. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, and then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030), and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap). With EV/EBITDA, this is the enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted, which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

I have slightly revised the assumptions embedded in the DCF model towards greater conservatism. I forecast the revenue CAGR for 2026-2030 at 6% versus 12.6% for 2021-2025. Likewise, I expect that the revenue growth rate will gradually slow down with the saturation of the market and the gradual normalization of demand. The company will reach maturity by 2028-2029 and will no longer focus on growth but on maintaining the achieved market share and monetization. Besides, the company probably won't be able to grow revenue faster without new M&A deals. This will help increase the operating margin from 8.86% to 10.76% by 2030. Cost control will significantly affect the growth of the operating margin, but still, high competition will not allow it to grow faster. Capital expenditures will be kept in the range of 1.5-2% to maintain high business growth rates. Thus, the FCF yield for 2026-2030 will be about 6.20% versus 3.88% for 2021-2025. In fact, according to my forecast, from 2028 the company will focus not on quantity but on quality, trying to increase the marginality of the business and free cash flow.

I got the WACC for the company at 7.56% due to a beta of 0.95x, an equity risk premium (taken from Damodaran's table) of 4.3% for the USA, and a 4.34% risk-free rate, which I took as 10-year USA Government Bond Yields. The cost of debt was 6.5%. I use the latest TTM interest expense divided by the latest one-year quarterly average debt to get the simplified cost of debt.

Due to the growth of financial indicators, the multiples have decreased slightly. For many of them, the company's shares are currently trading at a discount to the sectoral medians. Stocks with parameters like those of DXP usually trade at large premiums to the market. I think the multiples will rise in the near future, especially if the company releases a strong Q1 report.

The DCF model unequivocally confirms my judgments. In the most negative scenario (with WACC rising and multiples falling), the fair value of the shares should be higher by 0-7% compared to the current market price. That is, there is no downside even in the worst-case scenario. In the basic scenario, the stock should be 11-15% more expensive, and in the positive scenario, it should be 22% more expensive. I upgrade the rating from Hold to Buy with a target price of $185 and an upside of 14.5%.

Moderate Buybacks with Focus on Balance Sheet

The debt burden on the business remains elevated due to frequent M&A transactions. By the end of 2025, the company's total debt amounted to $924.5 million, with cash in the accounts in the region of $303.8 million. This results in a net debt of $620.7 million. The debt/EBITDA ratio is only 4.13x, and the net debt/EBITDA is 2.74x. Operating profit exceeds interest expenses by almost 3 times. The company does not pay dividends, investing its free funds in business development and maintaining growth rates. DXP is holding buybacks, but in 2025 significantly fewer shares were bought back—only 0.2 million shares worth $17.0 million. Most likely, management is trying to buy back shares only on drawdowns.

Risks

The company's risks have not changed much since the last article. DXP is most dependent on a favorable macroeconomic environment, as recessions and stagnation in the economy lead to a reduction in orders and aggregate demand. DXP conducts a lot of M&A transactions to support growth, and this creates additional risks associated with the integration of purchased companies, overpaying for low-cost, and inflated expectations of synergy with new businesses. The other risks are standard, such as high competition, oil price volatility, and a high debt burden.

Final Thoughts

DXP is in a good financial position, and high demand allows for rapid growth in revenue and operating margins. High oil prices have also been playing into the hands of businesses since March. I expect the company to release a strong report for Q1, which will create additional interest in the stock, and its price will rise. That's why I'm upgrading the rating from Hold to Buy.

data.title: Dominion Energy: A Discounted Utility With A Premium Yield
data.description:
Dominion (D) will be reporting earnings on May 1, and right now is a good time to revisit the story, which continues to steadily power along. The stock screens attractively on both comparative multiple valuation and its dividend yield of 4.2%. With a clear capital plan stretching out to 2030, there is real visibility into the company's future growth potential and delivery of the EPS growth algorithm.

Business Profile

Dominion is a regulated utility primarily operating across Virginia and South Carolina. As a regulated utility, returns are much more stable than a standard operating business. The company is 95% state-regulated on its utility operations and has had a 10.0% consolidated rate base CAGR. That CAGR has actually stepped up from 7.5% just two years ago because of data center demand for electricity causing prices to go up. The company's simple strategy is to provide reliable and affordable service to customers.

As a result, the company has a very clear algorithm:

  • 5-7% annual EPS growth rate
  • ~4% dividend yield
  • 9-11% total shareholder return

Recently, with the onset of AI and data center demand increasing the price for electricity, you're looking at a more variable component to the top line than in prior years. 2023 through 2026 has seen heightened PJM prices. The PJM is the Pennsylvania-New Jersey-Maryland regional transmission organization (RTO) that dictates the movement of electricity across 13 states, including Washington, DC. This is the area in which Dominion operates.

Investors can actually monitor live PJM prices, as well as load, among other things like outages, and gain a better understanding of how to think about the base rate growth for Dominion. It should remain steady, albeit at a 10% annual growth from here.

The company has a clear capital plan through 2030. The point of this, quite simply, is to grow the rate base. As they grow the rate base, the more capital they have available to return to shareholders and fund even more projects in the future. The plan totals $64.7 billion, and it is largely concentrated with a $54.8 billion capital plan for Dominion Energy in Virginia and $7.6 billion in South Carolina. There are residual capital outlays for Contracted Energy and Corporate. The company will be focused on investing in grid reliability.

The company's capital plan is comprised of 63% core grid and gas infrastructure, 25% electric transmission and distribution, with the balance of 18% being gas generation. The balance is solar, nuclear, and offshore wind. There is clear visibility for rate growth into the end of the decade.

To double click on the wind exposure the company has, we turn to the Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind (CVOW) project. The first delivery of electricity in this project is in the current quarter—Q1 2026. The project is still under partial construction and will not be completed until early 2027. This project carries a capital budget of $11.5 billion and requires $1.2 billion of additional funding from here. The next steps for the project are the installation of transition pieces, cable installation, installation, and commissioning of the wind turbine generators, and other transmission-related activities.

With a diversified capital plan for the next five years, investors can have confidence in both dividend safety and that the algorithm will be hit.

Financial Profile and Comparative Valuation

To build a forward estimate profile for Dominion, we need to start with the base rate trajectory. Virginia and South Carolina have lower electricity costs than the national average. Dominion notes the national average is around 17.8 c/kWh, while their business in Virginia charges 17.1 c/kWh and their business in South Carolina charges 15.7 c/kWh. This is favorable because it means there is room for electricity base rates to grow towards the national average before they brush up against affordability issues. As I said before, this is compounding at a 10% growth rate per year.

From there, we can start to think about squaring their guidance. The guidance this year calls for 2026 operating EPS to be $3.45-$3.69 (midpoint $3.57). Dominion does not provide top-line or other expense guidance.

Street is looking for $4.26 billion in revenue in Q1 and a small beat versus Street at $4.30 billion. The company has been beating top-line frequently, primarily driven by data center demand. The largest cost buckets for the quarter are electric fuel and other energy-related purchases, as well as other operations and maintenance. Assuming stable costs, albeit rising for electric, I am holding margins steady into Q1. Flowing through the revenue beat, then, I arrive at $0.90 versus the street at $0.86 in EPS.

Dominion is continuing to post a strong track record in EPS beats, having not missed since 2023. The consistency should drive confidence for investors that this company can close its valuation gap to peers.

That prompts a comparative valuation analysis. I'm using the peer set of Duke Energy (DUK), Southern Company (SO), American Electric Power (AEP), and NextEra Energy (NEE).

  • NEE trades at 22.82x forward earnings and 15.6x forward EV/EBITDA.
  • DUK trades at 19.08x forward earnings and 11.5x forward EV/EBITDA
  • SO trades at 20.48x forward earnings and 12.8x forward EV/EBITDA
  • AEP trades at 20.93x forward earnings and 12.47x forward EV/EBITDA

These peers are relative to D trading at 17.33x forward earnings and 13x forward EV/EBITDA.

  • NEE EBIT margin 30.23% with EPS growth of 8%
  • DUK EBIT margin 26.98% with EPS growth of 5-7%
  • SO EBIT margin 26.72% with EPS growth of near 6%
  • AEP EBIT margin 25.25% with EPS growth of 7-9%

These are relative to D's EBIT margin at 32.88% and 5-7% earnings growth guidance. It's interesting to see a discounted multiple for Dominion despite better operating margins to a significant degree and similar earnings growth versus peers. The spreads between these companies should be much tighter, and the discount doesn't seem to be warranted.

Dominion has been steadily grinding higher since late 2023, just after the time the Fed Funds rate hit its peak for the cycle. As a reminder, utility stocks are heavily influenced by interest rates. The stock maintains an upward trend, positioned above its 200-day moving average, and based upon the comparative analysis I just outlined, there really should be scope for outsized capital appreciation versus peers as the discount is unwarranted.

I also find it interesting to look at Dominion on a dividend basis versus these peers. Lower yields in this space can attract discounted multiples. D presently yields 4.3%. This is relative to NEE at 2.7%, DUK at 3.33%, SO at 3.2%, and AEP at 2.85%. At nearly a point better yield than the closest competitor, it is very surprising to see this valuation disconnect. While there are still scars from Dominion having cut their dividend back in 2020, the trust concerns should be well behind the company, as considerable time has passed.

While the yield for Dominion is at a lower level than the past few years, it is still best in class versus peers.

Risks

Given the nature of regulatory utilities, upside is effectively capped. Trying to argue for large inflections in base rates or dramatic amounts of upside is simply not feasible. State regulators focus on required returns on equity in order to make sure consumers are not overcharged for the basic necessity that is electricity.

Utilities like Dominion are also highly sensitive to interest rates given large capital outlays and leveraged balance sheets. As rates come down, utility stocks can outperform, and vice versa. Stubbornly high rates could force multiple compressions. Interest costs, in particular, can hurt bottom-line earnings. Leverage for most businesses is somewhere in the 0-2x net debt/adjusted EBITDA range, but businesses in the utility sector can often have 4-5x net leverage and comfortably operate. Financial conditions pressure is a clear risk. Finally, while companies like Dominion have great visibility on future projects, these projects can go over budget and force more spending, which likely means more balance sheet pressure as financing costs spike.

Conclusion

Dominion has a clear trajectory for further growth with a stable capital plan through 2030 that should provide solid base rate growth, driving decent earnings growth and stable dividend growth. What's most surprising about this stock is the fact that it trades at a discount to its peer group but has better margins, a better dividend yield, and similar earnings growth, all the while maintaining the safety of a regulated utility. There is a clear opportunity for multiple upsides on this name to close the gap.

data.title: The Nightview Fund Q1 2026 - The Software Opportunity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from The Nightview Fund Q1 2026 Investor Letter.

The Software Opportunity: Too Much Fear, Not Enough Thinking

Over the past several months, a legitimate debate has emerged about AI business models.

If the cost of training and running AI falls dramatically — if “intelligence becomes a commodity,” as some have declared — then what happens to the companies that have been charging for it? This is a reasonable question.

The market’s answer, when that fear took hold, was to sell everything software-adjacent and ask questions later.

We think that reaction was both understandable and wrong for a reason that is easy to miss: the best software companies are not primarily selling AI. They are selling workflows, integrations, data networks, and the organizational infrastructure that modern enterprises cannot function without. AI makes these businesses more valuable, not less, because it accelerates the rate at which their platforms become indispensable.

Large enterprises have little incentive to rebuild software infrastructure for non-core functions, and the bar for displacing an entrenched incumbent is extraordinarily high. Switching costs, compliance requirements, and organizational inertia all conspire against it. Disruption will happen at the margins, but for the truly embedded software businesses, AI is less a threat than potentially a tailwind.

Axon Enterprise (AXON)

Axon is not a software company in the way most people use that term, which is precisely why we think it is one of the most underappreciated businesses in our portfolio.

It is the operating system of public safety in America and, increasingly, around the world. The company sells Tasers, body cameras, in-car cameras, and — this is the key — a cloud-based evidence management platform called Axon Evidence, which has become the de facto system of record for law enforcement agencies across the country.

The software business alone is impressive: high recurring revenue, long contract durations, minimal churn, and a customer base — police departments and government agencies — that, once converted, essentially never leaves. The hardware serves as a distribution mechanism for the software. As Axon expands into records management, computer-aided dispatch, and AI-powered video analysis, the addressable market expands dramatically.

We initiated our position in Axon in late February as the stock corrected sharply in the broader software selloff. At the prices we paid, we were acquiring a business with roughly 30% annual revenue growth, expanding margins, and a near-monopoly position in its core market — for a valuation that reflected significant investor skepticism.

ServiceNow (NOW)

If you want to understand why we are bullish on enterprise software despite the AI-disruption narrative, ServiceNow is the clearest expression of our thesis.

The platform is, in the most literal sense, the workflow engine of large enterprises. It manages IT service requests, HR processes, legal requests, facilities management, and, increasingly, the cross-departmental orchestration that large organizations require to function. Roughly 85% of the Fortune 500 uses ServiceNow in some capacity. The average customer has expanded their usage year over year, a pattern that has persisted for years running.

Our view is that AI does not threaten this business so much as it accelerates it.

ServiceNow has been embedding AI capabilities — including agentic automation that can resolve IT tickets without human intervention — directly into its platform. Each AI feature gives customers more reason to deepen their reliance on the platform. The result is a flywheel: more AI features drive more workflow automation, which drives more data into ServiceNow’s systems, which makes the AI smarter, which drives more expansion.

We added ServiceNow to the portfolio in mid-March after the stock had sold off more than 20% from its highs on fears that AI would enable customers to build their own workflow tools. We find this thesis unpersuasive. The switching costs are enormous, the ecosystem deep, and the platform increasingly central to how enterprises operate. The selloff was, in our view, a gift.

Shopify (SHOP)

Shopify is a business that the market has consistently struggled to value, and for understandable reasons: it does not fit neatly into a single category. It is part payments company, part software platform, part logistics network, and part financial services provider. The revenue mix has evolved steadily.

Margins compress and expand as the company invests in new capabilities. For investors who want a clean, simple model, Shopify is frustrating. For investors who can look past the quarterly noise and see what the business is actually building, it remains exciting.

The core thesis is simple: Shopify is becoming a commerce infrastructure layer for the modern internet. When a merchant starts a Shopify store, they are plugging into a system that handles payments, inventory management, fulfillment logistics, customer data, marketing automation, and increasingly, cross-border commerce. The platform is deeply sticky.

The AI story at Shopify is also compelling. The company has been building AI-powered tools for merchants — product descriptions, ad copy, customer service automation, pricing optimization — that are genuinely useful, not just marketing. As AI capabilities become part of the core platform, Shopify becomes more valuable to more merchants, which expands the addressable market and deepens the moat. We added Shopify to the portfolio in mid-March and see it as a long-term compounder.

Autodesk (ADSK)

Autodesk is the kind of business that gets less credit than it deserves because it is not flashy. It makes software for architects, engineers, and construction professionals. It has been in the business for over forty years. It is not, on the surface, a headline-grabbing story. But underneath the surface, Autodesk is one of the most durable software franchises in existence, and its transition over the past several years from a perpetual-license model to a subscription-based cloud platform has dramatically improved the predictability and quality of its earnings.

We initiated our Autodesk position in late February when the stock was trading at a meaningful discount to our estimate of intrinsic value, driven by broader software sector weakness. The subscription transition is essentially complete. Free cash flow is growing. The company is beginning to layer AI capabilities — particularly in generative design and automated compliance checking — that should drive further expansion in average revenue per user. This is a classic Nightview holding: durable competitive moat, recurring revenue, and a long compounding runway.

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the NITE ETF. This and other important information about the Fund is contained in the prospectus, which can be obtained at www.nightviewfund.com or by calling (866) 666-7156. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

NITE is distributed by Northern Lights Distributors, LLC, member FINRA/SIPC.

Nightview Capital, LLC is not affiliated with Northern Lights Distributors, LLC.

Important Risks

Investing involves risk, including loss of principal. There is no guarantee the fund will achieve its investment objective. As an actively-managed ETF, the Fund is subject to Management Risk, Equity Securities Risk, Market Risk, Mid-Cap Company Risk, New Fund Risk, Operational Risk, Sector Risk, Small-Cap Company Risk, Smaller Fund Risk, Trading Risk, Value Investing Risk.

ETFs are subject to advisory and other expenses, which will be indirectly paid by the Fund.

ETFs are subject to issuer risks and other risks specific to the Fund. Shares of any ETF are bought and sold at market price (not NAV), may trade at a discount or premium to NAV and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns.

The Fund may face more risks than if it were diversified broadly over numerous industries or sectors. An investment in the Fund is not a deposit or obligation of any bank, is not endorsed or guaranteed by any bank and is not insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (“FDIC”) or any other government agency.

The opinions expressed herein are subject to change without notice. The opinions referenced are as of the date of publication, may be modified due to changes in the market or economic conditions, and may not necessarily come to pass. Forward-looking statements cannot be guaranteed. Readers are cautioned not to put undue reliance on such forward-looking statements, which are not a guarantee of future performance, and are subject to a number of uncertainties and other factors, many of which are outside Nightview’s control, which could cause actual results to differ materially from such statements.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vertex Pharmaceuticals: Looking For Signs Of Success In Q1 Earnings
data.description:
It has been over four months since I last covered Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), where I discussed how the company was still the unquestionable leader in cystic fibrosis (CF) but was working to diversify into pain, kidney disease, and cell and gene therapies. At the time, I was bullish on the company's strong financial position to back several potential launches between 2026 and 2028. Although I have confidence in Vertex’s commercial prowess for CF, it is fair to question whether they could clone that dominance in a multi-franchise portfolio.

Well, that question has gone from theoretical to tangible. The company's CF business has continued to strengthen thanks to ALYFTREK's commercial launch and their expansion into new populations and geographies. At the same time, Vertex has stepped beyond CF, with JOURNAVX showing traction and CASGEVY making headway. More importantly, the company’s renal pipeline took a big step forward with Phase III data and a regulatory path ahead. So, Vertex is no longer defined by a single franchise but now has new products helping drive growth, and renal has the prospects to reshape the VRTX thesis. Sadly, it looks as if the market is not as bullish on Vertex’s ability to replicate their CF success in other arenas. I believe the company will show signs of a successful transition in their Q1 earnings on May 4th. I’m looking to add to my position as we approach the Q1 earnings report.

Likewise, I intend to discuss the dynamic between Vertex's CF franchise and their attempts to diversify into other arenas. Then, I will preview the company’s Q1 earnings and what investors should be looking for in the press release and earnings call. In addition, I will point out some downside risks that investors need to consider when managing their position. Finally, I take a look at VRTX's daily chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to grab some shares below my “Buy Threshold” of $443.07.

Cystic Fibrosis Is Still the Foundation

Vertex’s CF franchise is mature and very well established; however, that categorization fails to describe what is transpiring in this arena. Well, the company’s CF franchise continues to grow in terms of both the expanse and penetration of this business. The launch of ALYFTREK is driving a steady conversion from TRIKAFTA, supported by superior CFTR modulation and simpler once-daily dosing, which matter more for long-term disease management and patient adherence.

More importantly, Vertex continues to push the therapy into younger populations, where the clinical impact becomes even more pronounced. I also have to point out that the company is expanding their reach geographically and digging into rare mutation subsets that have little to no treatment options. So, Vertex is broadening and digging deeper into the CF market. What is more, the prevalent CF population is growing in part due to improved survival, so the market is still growing in structure.

In fact, the CF market is expected to go from ~$18B in 2025 to ~$156B in 2033, so Vertex can benefit from just trying to remain the top dog in a market that is projected to have over 30% CAGR.

Proving Their Abilities

Vertex is in the midst of building another layer on top of their legacy business. So far, I would say the company has demonstrated that it can launch, scale, and commercialize outside their CF niche. CASGEVY and JOURNAVX are leading the charge and are projected to generate over $500M in combined revenue in 2026. Indeed, that figure isn't jaw-dropping, but if all goes well, analysts have estimated peak sales for CASGEVY to be in the $3B–3.6B range, with JOURNAVX estimates that are roughly $3+B. I’m not claiming the company has outperformed expectations, but I believe they’ve done enough to say they’re capable of branching out into multiple arenas simultaneously.

Honestly, I thought JOURNAVX would be the bigger test as they attempt to penetrate the pain medication market. However, the company expects JOURNAVX's prescriptions to more than triple year-over-year after doubling their sales force and payer coverage, expanding to roughly two-thirds of insured lives in the United States. In addition, Vertex is increasing their direct-to-consumer marketing and pushing physician engagement, which are typical moves in the Big Pharma playbook. So, I would say that Vertex is making the proper moves to ensure they can replicate their commercial success outside of CF.

Therefore, I don't think it’s fair to say that adding renal products to their portfolio is too aggressive and that it will unravel the company's operations. Yes, they might have some growing pains in renal, but I wouldn’t label this endeavor as irresponsible.

The Renal Opportunity

Vertex’s renal pipeline looks like it could be a legitimate second franchise and not just a single product endeavor. Povetacicept is targeting IgA nephropathy, which has an estimated market of roughly 330K in the U.S. and Europe. The Phase III interim data showed a 52% reduction in proteinuria with a clean safety profile. This was strong enough to encourage the company to start moving towards approval. Some analysts have projected povetacicept’s peak sales to be $4B or more, with the entire renal portfolio potentially climbing above $10B if Vertex hits on multiple indications.

What is more, povetacicept is not limited to IgAN and has potential in primary membranous nephropathy as well as generalized myasthenia gravis. So, povetacicept has the prospect of being a franchise drug with its pipeline. Vertex also has inaxaplin for the APOL1-mediated kidney disease program, which has roughly 250K patients in the U.S. and Europe that have no approved therapies. I have not found a public consensus peak-sales figure for Inaxaplin specifically, but it would have first-in-class positioning, so it could still have blockbuster potential.

Looking at the renal portfolio catalyst figure above, we can see several catalysts listed between 2026 and 2027. So, we should have a clearer picture of whether the renal portfolio is capable of being another pillar that could match the CF portfolio over the next twelve months.

Q1 Preview

Heading into 2026, the Street’s expectations for Vertex are measured top-line growth paired with a slight bump in EPS for the full year.

For Q1, the Street projects the company to report roughly $3B, which would be over 8% year-over-year growth.

However, EPS growth is projected to be comparatively soft, with only 5.72% year-over-year growth for Q1 and low-to-mid single-digit growth until we get into 2027. Vertex is still in an investment-heavy mode due to spending on commercial expansion and keeping the pipeline moving forward. So, we should expect a suppression in the company’s profitability for 2026.

The CF franchise should remain the primary source of revenue thanks to a stable and growing base for 2026. However, investors should see the Q1 earnings report as a checkpoint for Vertex’s commercial ramp outside of CF.

Using some elementary calculations, we should be expecting roughly $100M–$130M in combined non-CF revenue, with anything above suggesting that CASGEVY throughput is outperforming and/or JOURNAVX scaling is outperforming. I would like to see JOURNAVX’s script volume in the ~180K–220K range, which, in my opinion, would indicate that adoption is accelerating thanks to expanded payer coverage and a bigger sales force. I would like to see JOURNAVX’s revenue climbing above $50M for Q1. CASGEVY coming in the $60M–$80M range would ease the concerns over logistical constraints, and we are seeing throughput. I would believe these numbers would put the company on track to hit the $500M+ non-CF revenue target for 2026 and validate Vertex’s diversification game plan.

If those trends hold up over 2026, Vertex would be setting up a more pronounced inflection into 2027 and potentially consistent earnings growth over the next 10 years.

Q1 Risks

Vertex’s transition, occurring across multiple fronts simultaneously, opens up a lot of risks that investors need to keep an eye on going forward. The company’s legacy CF business has to continue to perform to help support their commercial and R&D activities. I can accept sluggish numbers from JOURNAVX and CASGEVY, but soft numbers from CF are worrisome because the rest of the company is dependent on that reliable revenue. Investors should keep an eye out to see if the conversion to the ALYFTREK rate slows.

Certainly, investors need to check on JOURNAVX’s adoption and if it is accelerating as expected. Slower-than-expected script growth would raise concerns about Vertex’s ability to penetrate the pain market now that they have expanded payer coverage and increased their investment in commercialization. For CASGEVY, we need to see a bump in throughput now that they have more sites activated. A lack of an inflection would suggest there are some onboarding bottlenecks remaining. Admittedly, I don’t know what the company or Street analysts are expecting in Q1, but a slow start to 2026 would put some stress on the company in the back half to hit their targets, which could lead to some execution risks if they try to make up in the second half of the year.

Finally, investors need to remain vigilant on any pipeline update, especially anything concerning the renal pipeline. Any suggestion of delays could have a huge impact on the company’s near-term outlook and possibly damage the long-term growth narrative.

Despite these risks, I’m keeping my VRTX conviction rating at 4 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

My Plan

I’ll be honest and admit that I might be blindly confident that Vertex will continue to gain traction beyond rare disease, and povetacicept will be the cornerstone for their renal franchise. In a few years, Vertex may no longer be viewed as a CF company but will be a diversified biotech growing into a commercial juggernaut that delivers dependable growth. I expect the company’s Q1 earnings to have several hints of this potential, so I’m looking to add to my VRTX position ahead of May 4th.

Looking at VRTX’s daily chart, we can see that the ticker is trading at about $438 per share, which is below my “Buy Threshold” of $443.07. So, I should be ready to click the buy button if a strong setup begins to form.

Currently, the ticker is “very bearish” on the Go-No-Go indicator and is trading below the anchored VWAP from the proximal high, as well as the 200-Day EMA, which has acted as resistance. On the other hand, there is a strong uptrend ray from the August 2025 low that has supported the share price over the past couple of weeks, so we could see that uptrend be strong enough to push the ticker back above my Buy Threshold in the coming days. As a result, I’m going to commit to adding to my VRTX in the immediate term to make sure I get something before the Q1 earnings.

Indeed, I could wait and see if the Q1 earnings hit the Street’s expectations before committing to adding to my position, but I’m also concerned that the share price might not return below my buy threshold before the company runs into a major catalyst or another earnings report.

Looking at the list of anticipated milestones above, we can see several potent catalysts that are expected to transpire between now and the end of the year. These catalysts, in conjunction with positive earnings, could force me to reformulate my Buy and Sell levels higher at some point in 2026 or 2027.

Long-term, my strategy is to trade this position with the purpose of generating profit and building a “house money” core position that has been funded by the market to hold as a long-term investment.

data.title: Tesla: Bearish Reaction From 200-Day MA With Weak Relative Strength (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Tesla (TSLA), the first US mega-cap stock (the Magnificent 7 group), will report its Q1 2026 earnings results after the close of today’s (Wednesday, 22 Apr 2026) US trading session.

The consensus forecast anticipates a slight increase in earnings growth, from $ 0.27 to $0.35 earnings per share (EPS) in Q1, representing a 30% rise over the same quarter a year ago.

Tesla has lagged the market and is the worst performer among the Magnificent 7

Since the ceasefire of the US-Iran war on 8 April 2026, the bullish animal spirits have reemerged in the US stock market, as the Magnificent 7 group of mega-cap stocks rallied, taking reference from the pre-war baseline of 27 February 2026 to Tuesday, 21 April 2026, led by Amazon (+19%), Nvidia (+12.8%), and Microsoft (+8%).

In contrast, Tesla underperformed, with a loss of 4%, and it also underperformed year-to-date (as of April 21, 2026), with a double-digit loss of 14.1% (see Fig. 1 & Fig. 2).

Medium-term technical outlook of Tesla (1 to 3 weeks)

Bearish bias below 417.40 key medium-term pivotal resistance. A break below 363.80 intermediate support opens the scope for further potential weakness to expose the medium-term supports at 337.25/328.20 (also the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of prior up move from 7 April 2025 low to 22 December 2025 high) and 300.05/288.80 (also the 76.4% Fibonacci retracement of prior up move from 7 April 2025 low to 22 December 2025 high) (see Fig. 3).

On the flip side, a clearance with a daily close above 417.40 invalidates the bearish scenario for a further recovery towards 437.40/450.20, followed by a potential retest at its current all-time high of 498.83.

Key elements to support the medium-term bearish bias on Tesla

  • The price actions of TSLA since 18 December 2024 high of 488.54 has started to form an impending major “Double Top” bearish reversal configuration.
  • The recent 21% rebound from its 7 April 2026 low has stalled and shaped a bearish reaction right after a retest on the key 200-day moving average.
  • The daily volatility-adjusted relative strength (VARS) of Tesla against the S&P 500 exchange-traded fund has trended lower below its zero line since 26 January 2026, which suggests the ongoing medium-term underperformance of TSLA against the SPY remains intact.
  • The daily RSI momentum indicator of TSLA has just staged a bearish reaction from its descending resistance at the 60 level, which suggests the lack of upside momentum.

Original Post

data.title: Schrodinger: AI Threat Is Looming Large (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Growing concerns around AI have weighed on Schrödinger's (SDGR) share price in recent months, a situation that has been exacerbated by relatively weak software growth and guidance for 2026. Doubts seem to be springing largely from the progress that Isomorphic Labs is making with its AI models.

I previously suggested that the decline in Schrödinger's stock was being driven by temporary market headwinds. At the time I felt the company's valuation was compelling, though, despite the potential for further downside in the near term. The stock is down around 25% since then.

While Schrödinger's software is unlikely to be displaced in the short term, there appears to be a realistic scenario where its use is limited to certain niches. The downstream value that the company is in line to receive probably more than justifies its current market capitalization, but it is difficult to see investor perception of the business changing anytime soon.

Market Conditions

Schrödinger continues to face a challenging demand environment (elevated interest rates, tight biopharma budgets, academic funding pressure), but recent share price weakness is probably being driven by concerns around long-term competitive positioning. This primarily relates to Isomorphic Labs recent Drug Design Engine (IsoDDE) announcement. IsoDDE builds on AlphaFold 3, improving accuracy and generalizability. It also predicts small molecule binding affinities with accuracies that exceed physics-based methods while being faster and lower cost.

Schrödinger Business Updates

While the tangible benefits of Schrödinger's software have already been demonstrated (accelerated development timelines and improved probability of success), there is a growing question around the long-term impact of AI. AI is also a force multiplier for Schrödinger's software, though, enabling companies to explore more chemical space. Despite this, there is a growing risk of displacement long-term.

Schrödinger believes that the data AI models are trained on is a significant limitation. Protein structure output from experimental methods and AI can be low-resolution, with Schrödinger suggesting that the structure of only around 10-15% of the human proteome is known with high resolution. The ability to accurately predict novel structures has also been limited so far. While Schrödinger is working on methods to improve the resolution of structures produced using AI, IsoDDE suggests that many of the limitations of AI are addressable.

Concerns about displacement by AI have likely been exacerbated by the recent weak financial performance of Schrödinger's software business. In response, Schrödinger is now emphasizing expansion opportunities, including materials science. Materials science revenue totaled 17 million USD in 2025, up from 15 million USD a year earlier. Applications include consumer packaged goods, chemicals, and electronics, with battery chemistry a particular focus area.

Within drug discovery, Schrödinger is working on areas like toxicology, synthetic chemistry, drug formulations, and improved support for biologics. Newly released products include:

  • Predictive toxicology for drug safety
  • Support for biologics
  • Crystal structure prediction that supports formulation
  • Retrosynthetic analysis
  • Agentic AI capabilities

Schrödinger's toxicology solution is now out of beta, and feedback has been strong. Toxicology remains an important challenge in drug discovery and often results in significant time and resources being wasted, meaning this solution could be a growth driver going forward. There are something like 20,000 proteins for which avoiding binding is desirable, though, and Schrödinger's software can only handle around 60-70 proteins at the moment.

Within the Drug Discovery business, Schrödinger plans on completing the Phase I studies of SGR-1505 and SGR-3515, with SGR-3515 data expected in the second quarter. Schrödinger has deemphasized its internal programs as collaboration demand has rebounded, though, and these programs are expected to be advanced through partners.

Schrödinger is also now emphasizing its "Modality Switch" programs, which have the same biological target but a different therapeutic modality, reducing biological risk. Schrödinger has already successfully discovered oral versions of injectable antibodies and peptides. Oral versions of antibodies and peptides are a large opportunity (tens of hundreds of billions of dollars) and can improve accessibility and costs. For example, cardiometabolic drugs like Mounjaro and Wegovy and inflammatory and immunology drugs like Humira.

On the partnership front, there are 20 separate collaborators and 16 programs for which Schrödinger is eligible for royalties on sales, 5 of which are targeting 5+ billion USD markets. Pharma royalties are generally in the mid-single- to low-double-digit range. Schrödinger is also eligible for up to 5 billion USD in milestones and owns a small piece of several successful biopharma companies. Schrödinger has already generated value from its equity stakes in Nimbus, Relay, Morphic, and Petra and retains equity positions in Nimbus, Ajax, and Structure Therapeutics.

Financial Analysis

While software revenue increased 11% in 2025, it was down 13% YoY in the fourth quarter, and ACV was fairly flat. Q4 2025 was weaker in terms of multi-year on-prem deals, which have an outsized impact on revenue. Software growth is being driven by hosted and maintenance revenues, although the transition towards hosted software is a headwind in terms of revenue recognition.

Total ACV grew 4% in 2025 to 198.5 million USD, with larger customers driving growth. Gates Foundation, Government/Academic customers, and smaller life sciences customers weighed on ACV growth in 2025, though. Schrödinger is focused on growing adoption within customers so that they are spending more than 1 million USD annually. Net dollar retention fell to 100%, after several years averaging above 110%, which Schrödinger believes is largely related to macro conditions. Gross dollar retention is 96%, though, indicating that churn is modest. It is worth noting that 2 of Schrödinger's largest customers were acquired in 2025, and while this is not expected to impact spend, it is a risk.

Schrödinger expects 218-228 million USD ACV in 2026, an increase of 10-15% YoY, and is targeting 10-15% annual software ACV growth over the next few years. Drug Discovery revenue is expected to be 55-65 million USD in 2026, and Schrödinger doesn't appear to be expecting material growth from its Drug Discovery business going forward.

Schrödinger is transitioning customers from on-prem to hosted deployments, which is expected to be a material revenue headwind over the next few years. Roughly a quarter of Schrödinger's software revenue is from hosted deployments at the moment, and the company is targeting 75% by 2028. Each 1% increase in hosted revenue contribution is expected to reduce revenue by 2-3 million USD in the current year. The impact of this will be more pronounced in 2027 and 2028, with revenue expected to begin converging with ACV by 2028.

Schrödinger's software gross margin declined to 74% in 2025. Drug Discovery margins have rebounded somewhat with scale, but the mix shift towards Drug Discovery has been a headwind. The transition to hosted revenue is also weighing on gross margins at the moment.

Schrödinger's operating expenses totaled 310 million USD in 2025, a decline of 9% YoY. This was driven by cost-cutting efforts and the transition of R&D expenses to COGS as the Drug Discovery business grows. Sales and marketing expenses were up modestly, though, with Schrödinger investing in long-term software growth. Schrödinger also reported a 65 million USD other income gain, which was largely related to mark-to-market changes in its equity investments.

Operating expenses are expected to decline again in 2026. Despite this, Schrödinger isn't expecting to achieve adjusted EBITDA profitability until 2028. This situation isn't as bad as it seems, though, as Schrödinger has so far avoided significantly diluting investors through downstream value creation.

Conclusion

Schrödinger's stock has been crushed by AI concerns in recent months, a situation that has been exacerbated by relatively weak software growth. While AI is a real risk, I still believe that Schrödinger is deeply undervalued. Even if the worst AI fears come true, displacement is likely to occur fairly slowly

The company only has a little over 900 million USD market capitalization, which is modest considering it generated over 250 million USD revenue in 2025 and has over 400 million USD cash. In addition, Schrödinger still stands to receive significant value from milestones and royalties.

Even though I believe that Schrödinger is likely to reward shareholders at some point, it is unclear what the catalyst will be for a turnaround at this point. This means that patience is required, and in the meantime, AI risk is likely to grow.

data.title: U.K. Inflation Heads Towards 4%, But Rate Hikes Off The Table For Now
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK

The latest rise in UK headline CPI tells us virtually nothing about the scale and duration of the inflation wave to come. The Bank of England is still flying blind, with the conflict unresolved. But the limited amount of survey data available so far suggests little cause for alarm on inflation. And against a fragile jobs market, we don’t expect a rate hike next week or this year.

As for that March data, there was nothing too surprising. The combination of higher motor fuel and heating oil prices added roughly 40bp to headline inflation, which now sits at 3.3%. Services inflation was up, though only because of volatile airfares; our estimation of the Bank of England’s preferred gauge of “core services” stayed at 4.2%.

On ING’s base case for oil and natural gas prices – which has the former staying between 90 and 100 USD/bbl in the second and third quarters of the year, and the latter averaging 55 EUR/MWh this quarter – we’d expect UK inflation to peak fractionally above 4% in August/September but generally bouncing around a 3.5-4% range in the second half of the year. That’s consistent with a 25% rise in household electricity/gas bills when the Ofgem price cap is next updated in July. And these forecasts assume some uptick in food inflation later this year.

Wholesale natural gas prices close to pre-war levels

Since we last updated our scenarios, natural gas prices have come under renewed downward pressure. And remarkably, futures prices for delivery 12 months into the future are within spitting distance of their pre-war levels. And this matters for the Ofgem price cap, which tries to mimic what energy providers pay to lock in future supplies. If wholesale prices stay where they are today, the July price cap is more likely to rise by a mere 10-15% and retrace much of that increase in October. That’s consistent with inflation peaking around 3.5%.

So long as inflation doesn’t spike materially above 4% – a level above which the Bank has identified as being more likely to trigger a persistent bout of price pressure – we think the BoE will prefer to keep rates on hold this year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Red Cat: A High-Risk Bet On The Future Of Warfare
data.description:
Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (RCAT) is seeing tremendous growth. The company is targeting a fast-growing drone market that continues to see an increase in demand. The growth and market in which Red Cat operates is what drew me to the stock in the first place and why I gave it a buy rating when I previously reviewed the stock. The stock has performed well since I last reviewed it to start the year. A lot changes in a few months with a company moving as fast as Red Cat. Furthermore, world events are keeping investors on their toes, particularly with defense stocks.

Recent global conflicts are increasing demand and the need for their technology and products. The company saw revenues increase to a point of scale in the fourth quarter. The company has grown its manufacturing capabilities in an impressive way. It has come at a cost to margins, but it has positioned itself to capitalize on future growth.

Margins and expenses continue to be a concern for the company. It will be something to continue to watch as the company starts to produce at scale. At current margins and expense rates, the company will take years to get itself to a point of profitability.

There are risks, as the company is burning cash and is a long way from profitability. I think overall, the market trends and strength outweigh the risks. The demand is growing, and the company has positioned itself to take advantage of that growth.

Financials

The company reported a large step up in revenue and volume during Q4. Revenue in Q4 was $26.2 million; this is up from $1.3 million in the prior year. The more impressive part was the growth sequentially from Q3, which was 172%. For the full year, the company reported revenue of $40.7 million, an increase of 161% from the prior year. Q4 was really the quarter where the company scaled production, and revenues showed the result.

While the revenue growth was fantastic, the gross margin continued to be a major concern. The company reported a gross margin of 4.2% for the quarter. This is really bad when you consider that management guided for a gross margin of around 10% for the quarter. This is also down sequentially, even though the company did a much higher volume. I attribute this to the costs of getting the business to the scale it is now operating at. The company has built out its capacity in a drastic way over the past year. The gross margin has suffered during that time, but I expect the company to begin to work at scale and incorporate efficiencies into the process. I am not as worried about the gross margin right now due to the stage the company is at, but it will quickly become a concern if improvement is not seen over the second half of 2026. A low gross margin is going to make it difficult to ever turn a profit. The company had previously guided for a gross margin in the low 20s for 2026. I am not that optimistic after Q1. I think it will be much more likely closer to 10%-15% for the year.

The other concern is related to high operating expenses. Those operating expenses continued to increase at a rapid rate as well. Operating expenses increased to $25 million during the fourth quarter, up from $18.2 million in Q3. Most of that is related to G&A expenses as well, with it reaching $16.5 million. I understand that the company is in rapid growth mode, but those expenses continue to expand at a fast rate, especially the G&A expenses. Although it is a much slower pace than revenue. One thing that is trending down at a rapid rate is the operating expense as a percentage of revenue. This is the first quarter where revenues exceeded operating expenses. In Q3, operating expenses were double revenues. So the company is trending in the right direction and quickly. It has a long way to go, though. The operating expenses plus low gross margin are going to make it tough to become profitable.

In my previous article, I did a high-level estimate on how long it might take for the company to be profitable and also how much revenue it would require based on gross margin and current operating expenses. The profitability point got pushed out even further based on the current quarterly results. Based upon a 30% gross margin, the company's long-term goal, and annualizing the current operating expenses, the company would have to generate revenue of approximately $335 million to break even. The company is seeing strong revenue growth, but that revenue figure is a long way out. The company either needs to improve the gross margin or reduce its operating expenses as it goes forward.

The company has done multiple capital raises during the year. This has diluted shareholders, but it has put the company in a good cash position. The company ended the quarter with $167.9 million in cash. This gives the company flexibility and time to build its operations and get to a point of positive cash flow. The company also has options with M&A with the cash position.

Blue Ops

The Blue Ops business has developed at a rapid rate. This division was launched last year. The company really dug in and got to work getting a product designed and developed. They started demonstrating their USV at the end of last year. That is quick development and completion from the company. The company then got a lease on a 155,000-square-foot factory and converted it to manufacture their boats. Management noted during the earnings call that they expected to be at full-rate production by the end of March. They also noted, “We believe and are confident that they can build over 100-plus USVs in 2026 as we ramp up production capability to thousands.” The company expects to deliver the first USV boat in Q2. To go from the idea to a year later being at full-rate production in a year's time is very impressive.

The company held its innovation day at the facility. It also demonstrated some of their USV technology at the event. It really shows me the capabilities that have been built into the unit in the past year. This operational unit was a bit of a wildcard to me when I previously reviewed the stock, but it is developing much faster than I had anticipated. It looks like it should begin to add to revenues in 2026 at a much higher rate than I expected.

Drones

The company has had some positive and not-so-positive news come out of the drone business over the past few months. The company is starting to see more adoption of its drones around the world, particularly in Ukraine. The company also received some bad news about missing the cut for the Drone Dominance Program.

We can start with the bad news. I discussed at length the Drone Dominance Program in my last article. I will keep it brief here. This is, in essence, a program for the Department of Defense to source domestically manufactured drones. It is a competition where drone manufacturers are invited to have their drones tested by the military. The first stage had 11 companies chosen and receive orders for drones. Red Cat was not selected as one of the companies for Gauntlet I of the program. This is a big hit for the company. We are talking about tens of millions in steady revenues. This would have helped the company continue building out its drone business.

The company can and still plans to compete in Gauntlet II of the program. Just because the company was not selected in Gauntlet I does not mean it will not be selected in Gauntlet II. It is also important to note that just because it was not selected as part of the Drone Dominance Program does not mean that the company will not receive orders through the military. Winning in that program would have been a boost for the business for sure. It would have locked in a large amount of orders. But it does not mean that it is not going to win other contracts and programs outside the Drone Dominance Program. I do think it will be more difficult in Gauntlet II by missing out on Gauntlet I. This could turn out to be a longer-term miss for the company.

The company deployed a team to Ukraine with their Black Widow drone. The team was looking to get Black Widow into the field and make sure it is capable of doing what it is supposed to do. They are hoping to replace some Chinese-made drones that are being utilized in the country. The company's COO discussed more about this on the call, as he had returned from Ukraine the day before. It was noted that their products were being used in battle and were performing well. In fact, the company has received a letter of request from Ukrainian forces to provide the Black Widow drone. The company has established an office in Ukraine to meet the constant needs of the market there.

Manufacturing Capacity

Manufacturing capabilities were a big topic of discussion. The company really ramped up production capacity, which was seen during Q4. The company has grown its facility capacity from 36,000 square feet two years ago to over 254,000 square feet today. The other key part of this is that the company has an NDAA-compliant supply chain. This allows it to compete on many contracts that others cannot. Their USA manufacturing and sourcing have started to help set the company apart from certain competitors.

The company has expanded its Black Widow drone facility in Utah. They hit a capacity of 50 drones manufactured a day. The company expects to be at a manufacturing capacity of 1,000 Black Widow drones a month in the first half of the year. It was noted that this capacity is done on one shift. The company estimates it can triple the number of manufacturing lines and also add more shifts.

The Flightwave facility has the capability to manufacture 130 drones per month. That is only using ⅓ of the available space as well.

I discussed Blue Ops already, but the company noted that they have room to expand their capacity at their current facility to 100 boats a month.

The key with all these manufacturing facilities is that the company has ramped up capabilities significantly in a short time and positioned itself for growth. The growth will not come at as high an expense either. They do not have to build new facilities; they already have the facilities and only have to expand production. This massive buildout is also a contributor to a lower gross margin. I fully expect to start to see the gross margin increase over the second half of the year. Now that the buildout is there, it can begin to work on scale and efficiency.

Outlook

The company declined to provide annual guidance during the quarterly announcement and ensuing call. The company got burned pretty good last year on the guidance provided. I think there was a bit of a learning curve with government contracts and the amount of time it takes to get those deals done. I know this was a concern for many investors, but it does not bother me too much. The company is new in the industry, and it takes some time to learn the government sales pipeline and convert that into dollars. I don't think there is any value in providing inaccurate estimates. There is no indication that the lack of guidance was due to a slowing business. Management noted they expect continued growth during the year.

During the Q&A, there were questions around revenue estimates. Management noted the following: “There's a range from all of you out there between $100 million and $170 million…. We're comfortable in the top half of that, but we're not ready to commit to it until we get contracts in hand to give official guidance.” Based upon this commentary, analysts adjusted their estimate a bit higher than they were previously and now have the company at $148.8 million for 2026.

The other item that came out of the call was the actual volume of drones being used in Ukraine. I have been covering multiple drone companies, and there is not a lot of information provided on volumes and market potential. Everyone knows there is demand, but how much? The COO stated that Ukraine told them they are going through 350,000 ISR drones per year. That is a massive number and market potential. Red Cat is producing 1,000 drones a month. The company cannot even produce a tiny fraction of the number of drones being used in Ukraine per year. It does not even make a dent in the demand. This shows the demand and size of the market potential within the drone industry.

The company also still has its SRR program, which they expect to enter full-rate production soon. Management also noted that they expect orders to come out of Operation Epic Fury for the drone business. Management also noted they are currently building 1,000 drones a month. This is due to the demand they are already seeing in the market.

The Strait of Hormuz and issues currently happening there have brought a lot of added attention to the Blue Ops division of the business. Management noted that they are seeing increased demand from Gulf States as well as the Navy. Managing and controlling the Strait of Hormuz has become a critical talking point. Red Cat's technology provides the option of using USVs to patrol the Strait to provide counterprotection from the shore of Iran. I expect the company will generate more revenues from the Blue Ops division than I anticipated, and it could easily push the company to beat annual estimates for the year. The company previously noted that they expect revenue of $750k to $1.5 million for each vessel. The company was going to use its first 15 vessels for demonstrations, but due to demand, they now plan to ship those to customers. The company could end the year with 25+ sales, providing a boost to revenues in 2026.

Valuation

The company is valued based on future potential. Its current financials leave some wanting with the losses that I don't expect to turn positive for some time. The revenue growth and market demand are really strong enough to offset the negative profits.

The company has a market cap of $1.54 billion. The P/S ratio on the TTM is 30.9. That is a high P/S, but the company is just getting started on its growth stage and reaching scale. Revenues are expected to grow by over 250% for the year. This brings the P/S down really quickly, with a 2026 expected P/S of 10.36. I also think the revenue estimates are conservative. We have already seen analysts raise estimates since I wrote my last article and noted that revenue estimates were too low. I still think the company will beat the current revenue estimates, making the multiples lower.

The company operates at a loss, and as I noted in the calculation on earning a profit, it is likely to continue to have losses for multiple years. The valuation is rich for the company. I also think the market potential makes this rich valuation more reasonable. While it trades at high multiples and is operating at a loss, the company is really just getting started in the market and has high growth potential going forward.

Conclusion

Red Cat has seen its revenues and market grow drastically. It has started to reach scale with its revenues. Those revenues are expected to continue to grow at a rapid pace in 2026, with expectations of over 250% revenue growth. This growth is being driven by the overall market in which Red Cat operates. Epic Furry and the oil crisis really drive home the demand that countries will have for the technology and products that Red Cat offers. War is evolving, and the company has positioned itself well to be a part of that evolution.

The company has large losses as it operates on a thin gross margin and has high operating expenses. The gross margin is something to watch seriously in the second half of the year. As the company begins to manufacture at scale, I would expect this to start to increase. With the thin gross margins and high operating expenses, the company will have to continue strong growth for multiple years before looking at a profit. This is the biggest concern with the company at the moment. It does have a strong cash position to last while the company works towards profitability.

While the valuation is not cheap for the company and it has plenty of risks, I think the long-term market potential outweighs the risks at this point. The market demand is there and will only continue to grow. I previously entered a position when I covered the stock and have seen the stock climb since then. I think it is still worth a position as an investment in the drone market. Likewise, I rate the company a buy.

data.title: Salesforce: AI Is A True Acceleration Driver, Not A Hollow Rebrand
data.description:
While it’s true that the stock market as a whole has shaken off the rising geopolitical tensions in Iran plus a weak macro in rather quick fashion, the notion of the “SaaSpocalypse” is still very much alive, and in the software sector, a deep bear market has escaped very few stocks.

Salesforce (CRM), often considered the pioneer of cloud applications technology and a premier leader in the space, hasn’t been spared. Long a growth stock that traded at heady revenue multiples, Salesforce’s ~30% decline since the start of the year has now positioned the stock at earnings and cash flow multiples that are well below overall S&P 500 averages. The question is, is this a permanent re-rating for Salesforce, and can it thrive in the AI revolution?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Salesforce in January, when the stock was still trading above $240 per share. Since then, Salesforce has continued a frustrating slide downward, even as founder and CEO Marc Benioff continues to assure the market that it’s wrong on the endgame impacts of AI on software. Amid the sliding valuation on top of clear evidence that Salesforce is not facing wholesale disruption, I’m reiterating my "Buy" opinion on Salesforce.

Zooming Into Salesforce’s Pivot to Agentforce

It’s now impossible to discuss the prospects of any software company without first discussing the “SaaSpocalypse” and what impacts AI will have, if any, on the business.

I’ll back up for a minute to my broader macro view: In my opinion, the SaaSpocalypse is overstated. AI will have an impact on software company business models, but the impact will be more limited and selective - constrained largely to companies that primarily price their products on a per-seat basis.

While Salesforce’s legacy cloud products like CRM and even newer acquired products like Slack are seat-based, Salesforce has two things going for it. The first is that Salesforce is a large enterprise business. The companies that drive Salesforce’s revenue, including those in highly regulated industries like big banks, have Salesforce products deeply ingrained into everyday operations. It’s simply not realistic or even cost-efficient to rip out the software tools that are so integrated into internal and third-party systems to replace them with agentic AI tools.

Note as well that software frequently has issues, and part of the deal with an annual subscription package is ongoing professional support and maintenance. AI has reduced the barriers to entry and cost for building apps, but it doesn’t really move the needle on the economics of build vs. buy.
